question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on all good relations funding across their Department.
answer_text: We are committed to building a better future for everyone which is why The Executive Office has invested over £24.5m to support good relations programmes this current financial year. This investment includes a wide range of programmes including the Central Good Relations Fund, Urban Villages and T:BUC Camps Programmes.Since the Central Good Relations Fund launched in 2016, we have invested over £30.5M, supported over 950 projects.Over the next four years a further £18.5m in match funding is being provided to PeacePlus programmes. We know this work is having a positive impact.96% of participants in Central Good Relations Fund Programmes felt their project helped bring people of different backgrounds together.78% of respondents reported feeling more knowledgeable about different cultural traditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of whether the aims of those campaigning for Caoimhé's Law can be progressed in legislation within the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: The decision to introduce legislation to prohibit the sharing of photos of road traffic collisions would be a matter for the Department for Infrastructure, which has primary responsibility for road safety policy within the Executive.  However, I recognise that this is an important issue that also cuts across the responsibilities of many Departments and organisations, including my own, and I am committed to ensuring that my Department engages to consider how best we might provide support.  Therefore, I have agreed to participate in a proposed roundtable discussion convened by Road Victim Support Northern Ireland – Donegal, to consider appropriate measures to prevent the unauthorised recording and sharing of photos of road traffic collisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of the latest housing waiting list figures in the Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council; and (ii) to detail any action his Department will take to ensure waiting lists and the reliance on temporary accommodation in this local council area are reduced.
answer_text: I have been clear about the challenges we are facing in terms of social housing.  We need to reduce the numbers of people on the waiting list for social housing and the use and cost of temporary accommodation, not just in Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council, but across Northern Ireland. Increased housing supply more generally is fundamental to addressing these challenges. The Executive Housing Supply Strategy sets out the long-term framework for delivering the supply we need across NI. My Department is currently leading on work, in collaboration with other Departments and the NIHE, to produce the first action plan detailing a range of actions to be delivered in implementing the Strategy. Some of the actions to address these issues are an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive’s strategic approach to responding to homelessness and reducing the need for temporary accommodation is outlined in the Homelessness Strategy 2022-27 and the Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation.”“Additionally, the new build programme continues the support of growth of social rented stock in the Council area. In Armagh, Banbridge and Craigavon: There have been 30 new social housing Completions 2025/26.” “There are currently 248 social housing units under construction.” “There are 105 social housing units programmed to start as part of the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) 2025/26 & 2026/27 – 2027/28.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the budget allocated to NI Water in 2025; and (ii) to detail how the budget, allocated to NI Water this year, will help unlock housing development.
answer_text: i) The tables below confirm NI Water’s Final Opening 2025-26 Budget positions, for both resource and capital and its June Monitoring outcome.Capital2025-2026£kRRI Financed – Ring-fenced105,700General allocation244,300Final Opening 2025-26350,000June Monitoring Allocation – Ring-fencedIncreasing wastewater capacity11,300Urban Drainage Transformation Pilot750TOTAL BUDGET ALLOCATION362,050Resource2025-2026£kOpening resource DEL149,230Final Opening 2025-26149,230Increased employers’ NIC1,030June Monitoring – general allocation including towards increased employer’s NIC costs1,000TOTAL BUDGET ALLOCATION151,260NI Water has received 23% of the opening resource and over 40% of the general capital budgets allocated to the Department. At opening budget, NI Water has received 86% of what it identified it could deliver this year, and when the additional funding that was secured in June monitoring is taken into consideration, this increases to almost 90% of the ask, which is a significant level of investment in our water and wastewater infrastructure.(ii) Following June Monitoring this year, I allocated £11million to NI Water to unlock 3,000 houses in the Derry area. This means since my predecessor John O’Dowd and I came into office, we have provided ringfenced allocations to enable the unlocking of wastewater capacity for over 5,000 properties across the North. This is already above the 4,300 that NI Water had originally planned to connect, if fully funded, by the end of this Price Control Period in 2028, and it is only 2025.Furthermore, I have consistently said that innovative thinking and working in partnership hold the key to unlocking wastewater constraints. The recent announcement connecting 400 properties in Newry, shows what can be achieved when we challenge traditional thinking and focus on smarter, more sustainable solutions, which lies at the heart of my three-pronged approach. Having been effectively closed to significant new development due to long-standing wastewater infrastructure constraints, my department’s action means that vital economic growth in Newry will now be unlocked, thanks to this new and dynamic alternative to traditional upgrade methods.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact on domiciliary care packages, as a result of the independent sector staff not receiving an uplift to the real Living Wage.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the work being undertaken in her Department to better support taxi drivers.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the regulation of the taxi industry in relation to taxi driver, operator and vehicle licencing. It has two main functions that currently impact on the availability of taxi drivers: setting maximum fares and awarding taxi drivers’ licences. To that end the maximum fare was increased in December 2023 and, since the introduction of the test for taxi drivers, the Driver and Vehicle Agency has made several changes to the theory test including lowering the overall pass mark and providing revision guides.To help address the various challenges currently facing the taxi industry, I also announced earlier this year a phased review of taxi policy and legislation, commencing with a review of Class C Taxi Licensing. As part of this process, I have engaged directly with representatives from the taxi sector to gain a deeper understanding of their concerns.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 17336/22-27, (i) for an update on the progress of the trial scheme in Britain, as well as the impact of the recent legislative changes in the south; and (ii) to detail the engagement her Department has had with stakeholders on the enforcement of e-scooters on public roads.
answer_text: In the absence of any enabling legislation, it remains illegal to use e-scooters on public roads and public spaces here. I currently have no plans to change that position. It also remains illegal to use e-scooters on public roads or in public places in Britain unless they are being used as part of the ongoing Department for Transport (DfT) trial of rental e-scooters scheme. This scheme has been extended until May 2028. DfT may introduce legislation there once the pilot scheme is complete and its outcomes assessed.  As you are aware, legislation for e-scooters was introduced in the south in May 2024. A review of the impact of this legislation has not yet been published. Responsibility for enforcement of e-scooters on public roads rests with the PSNI.  To help inform the way forward here, my officials will continue to monitor the progress of the trial scheme in Britain as well as the impact of the recent legislative changes in the south.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the work to progress a permanent memorial to victims and survivors, as recommended by the Historical Institutional Abuse Inquiry report.
To ask the Minister of Education to provide an update on (i) his Department's; and (ii) the Education Authority's, plans for remedial and maintenance works at St Patrick's Grammar School, Armagh.
answer_text: Officials from my Department and the Education Authority (EA) are working closely together to review the significant issues at St Patrick’s Grammar School relating to mould remediation and upgrade of the heating works. Both are significant schemes and will inevitably involve significant disruption to the school, so officials are reviewing all options to ensure the preferred options provide value for money but also minimise disruption as much as possible. I understand that EA Maintenance has carried out emergency works to the heating system to sustain the system in advance of the main heating upgrade scheme.Officials will continue to engage with the school and keep them updated on these urgent works.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether additional funding has been considered to enable the office of the Independent Autism Reviewer to carry out independent research.
answer_text: My Department remains committed to delivering its commitments under the Autism Act (NI) 2011 (as amended).  Annual funding of £300k has been provided for the delivery of the Autism Strategy 2023 to 2028.  A proportion of this budget has been utilised to support the initial running costs for the Reviewer’s office.  In January 2025, my Department allocated a dedicated staffing resource to provide full time Head of Office support; this resource is being funded from within my Departmental admin budget and is not taken from the programme delivery allocation. The Reviewer’s Office has identified additional support requirements, including for staffing and to support research capacity. My officials will continue to engage with the Reviewer on this matter but it will be challenging given the level of financial pressures currently facing my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his Department's assessment of the new monitoring framework from the Independent Autism Reviewer.
answer_text: The Autism Strategy 2023 to 2028 sets out to deliver actions across Health, Education, Housing and Public Services including the Civil Service.  Significant progress and improved outcomes have already been made within the first two years of delivery.  This is reflected in the year two progress report on the second year of the 2023 to 2025 delivery plan which is now available on the Department of Health website. My officials have commenced work on the next delivery plan for 2025 to 2028.  A monitoring framework has been shared by the Autism Reviewer which will help inform and improve the delivery and monitoring of the final three years of implementation of the Strategy. A consultation workshop on the next delivery plan is scheduled for the 8 October 2025. This will provide an opportunity for all key stakeholders to agree actions and develop measurable targets for the remainder of the Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work his Department is doing to ensure the office of the Independent Autism Reviewer is sustainable.
answer_text: My Department remains committed to delivering its commitments under the Autism Act (NI) 2011 (as amended).  Annual funding of £300k has been provided for the delivery of the Autism Strategy 2023 to 2028.  A proportion of this budget has been utilised to support the initial running costs for the Reviewer’s office.  In January 2025, my Department allocated a dedicated staffing resource to provide full time Head of Office support; this resource is being funded from within my Departmental admin budget and is not taken from the programme delivery allocation. The Reviewer’s Office has identified additional support requirements, including for staffing and to support research capacity. My officials will continue to engage with the Reviewer on this matter but it will be challenging given the level of financial pressures currently facing my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action being taken within his Department to ensure there is a greater consistency of autism services across all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: There are significant variations in funding, service delivery models, and staffing across Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust autism services, which is resulting in inequities in access and support across different areas. My Department is committed to addressing these disparities and is undertaking a number of actions to improve the situation, within the constraints of the current financial environment.Children’s ServicesWe have recently launched a public consultation on a draft Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework, available on the Department of Health website. It is anticipated that the implementation of this framework will lead to greater consistency in approaches to care.In collaboration with the Public Health Agency and Child Health colleagues, the Department is consulting on the development of a regionally agreed, needs-based neurodevelopmental pathway. This will enable individual needs to be assessed within a consistent regional framework and will support earlier intervention without requiring a formal diagnosis. The framework is designed to align with NICE guidelines and will underpin the development of a clearly defined, regionally consistent neurodevelopmental pathway for children and young people.Adult ServicesIn March 2025, the Department appointed a dedicated Project Lead for Adult Autism to provide regional leadership and focus on enhancing services for autistic adults and their families across Northern Ireland.An initial scoping and engagement exercise has been completed with HSC Trusts to map current adult autism diagnostic and support pathways and a regional workshop was held in July 2025 to collectively explore what can be done within existing resources to manage growing waiting lists, reduce unwarranted variation, and better meet population needs.To improve consistency of adult autism diagnostic and support services across HSC Trusts, my Department is currently progressing regional work to review, update, and consult on a revised Adult Autism Care Pathway. This work, which has recently commenced, will involve comprehensive stakeholder engagement. It is anticipated that the resulting guidance will support the standardisation of adult diagnostic, support, and information pathways across the region.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the steps her Department is taking to assist phone network providers to navigate the process of network development, in order to improve connectivity.
answer_text: Thank you for your question which has been transferred to me from the Department for Infrastructure. Although mobile policy is a reserved matter and network development is delivered here on a purely commercial basis, there are several actions local departments can take to accelerate the rollout of mobile communications infrastructure. These actions were identified in the Mobile Action Plan (MAP) and include communications around the benefits of mobile technologies, the potential to use public assets to house mobile communications infrastructure and reviewing the planning process. These actions are being delivered via the Barrier Busting Taskforce (BBT). Minister Kimmins and I recently met with mobile industry representatives to discuss progress of the MAP actions and have agreed to refocus and energise efforts to accelerate delivery of actions that fall under our remit. I have also written to other key stakeholders to encourage them to commit appropriate resources to deliver the rest of the outstanding actions. From discussions with industry, it is clear that they feel planning reform is a key issue and although this will be considered by the Planning Subgroup within the BBT, I have been informed that Department for Infrastructure officials have commenced early policy development into reviewing the permitted development rights for telecommunications. This will involve stakeholder engagement and public consultation, at the appropriate time, including with the mobile industry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the current Shared Island funding within her Department.
answer_text: My Department has received Shared Island funding, along with future commitments, for a number of key infrastructure projects. These include: the A5 Western Transport Corridor; the Enterprise rail service between Dublin and Belfast; and the Ulster Canal restoration project which is being delivered by Waterways Ireland. In addition to this, a number of projects, which help to deliver the strategic objectives of my Department, have also benefited from Shared Island Funding, such as, the rollout of community focussed EV charging points and Active Travel Projects.These innovative and forward-thinking projects are a solid investment towards our ability to deliver the infrastructure of the future, and I will continue to engage closely with my counterparts in the South on further projects which benefit the island of Ireland.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the work of the Delivery Unit.
answer_text: This Executive continues to deliver against the priorities outlined in the Programme for Government. The Childcare Subsidy Scheme has saved eligible working parents approximately £8 million since it launched in September 2024. In Health, almost 59,000 additional outpatient, diagnostic and inpatient procedures have been delivered – which is well on track to meet the PfG target of 70,000.The first ever Executive agreed strategy for housing has been published, and implementation of the agreed Lough Neagh Action Plan and the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls programme are progressing well.		While much has been done, we know there is more to be done, both in the short term and in the medium to long term, to make sure that progress is maintained. This is why we have established a Delivery Unit – to keep a relentless focus on delivery against our priorities. The Unit will work across the wider Executive to understand the various challenges, review current plans, monitor progress and identify specific areas where we can support delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, for his assessment of the level of training and awareness for healthcare staff on the unique challenges faced by adopted children.
answer_text: Adopted children often have complex needs, which are best met by a multi-agency approach involving health, social care and allied health professionals. All professionals involved in this process require an understanding and awareness of the unique needs of adopted children. Medical students will encounter a range of scenarios and training opportunities relating to adopted children, during their undergraduate and postgraduate training programmes. These include:Lectures covering the topics of attachment, parenting, safeguarding, child/adolescent mental health and child protection are included on the undergraduate curriculum. During community paediatric and General Practice placements, students will have opportunities to meet adopted children or be present during adoption medicals.In relation to postgraduate medical training, the core paediatric training curriculum requires trainees to develop their knowledge of the current legal framework related to babies, children, young people, families and carers to their clinical practice (e.g. adoption and safeguarding).The specific needs of adopted children are also covered as a postgraduate topic for those training to specialise in paediatrics or child psychiatry.Specialist Community Public Health Nurses (SCPHNs) such as Health Visitors and School Nurses, provide support to families and children pre-and post-adoption and therefore also understand the unique challenges adopted children may face.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether additional training is in place for educational professionals to better understand and support adopted children.
answer_text: There is no specific training in respect of supporting adopted children, however education professionals will be able to access training via the Education Authority training calendar that can assist with supporting a child or young person’s presenting educational need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what support is in place in schools for adopted children.
answer_text: Post adoption support is provided through the local Health and Social Care Trusts in the first instance. Schools should work on an individual basis with children and families, when a need is identified that requires additional support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what engagement he has had with the Education Authority in relation to school placements for the start of the 2025-26 academic year.
answer_text: In response to the priority which I have placed on SEN placements, the Education Authority (EA) in February 2025 reconvened the SEN Placements Taskforce, chaired by the Chief Executive bringing together key officers from across the organisation alongside officials from my department.In addition, my officials continue to work collaboratively with the EA in ongoing efforts to secure sufficient and appropriate provision to support children and young people with SEN for September 2025. Through my officials, I receive regular updates on both the number of children with SEN who continue to require a placement for September 2025, and the operational efforts by the EA to identify solutions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister of Health in relation to communication between both Departments and statutory agencies on the supports and services that should be provided to children with a Statement of Special Educational Needs in Newry and Armagh.
answer_text: I am committed to working in partnership with the Minister of Health to improve outcomes for children and young people with special educational needs (SEN) and complex medical and healthcare needs. The Education Authority has advised that the Health SEN Co-Ordinator from the Southern Health and Social Care Trust meets with the Armagh Office Manager from the Statutory Assessment & Review Service monthly to discuss submission of medical advice for children undergoing statutory assessment. Representatives from the Statutory Assessment and Review Service meet with all of the Health SEN Co-ordinators every 6 weeks to discuss any emergent issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what plans are in place to ensure children in Newry and Armagh with a Statement of Special Educational Needs are placed in a suitable school in time for the start of the 2025-26 academic year.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:In advance of the 2025-26 academic year the EA continues to work to increase capacity by an additional two specialist provision classrooms subject to statutory approvals. Additionally, the EA has secured approvals to provide modular accommodation to Rathore Special School to improve existing class sizes. In so doing the EA will maximise provision across these localities to ensure children and young people can access education in line with their needs identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration he has given to a Special Educational Needs first school placement system.
answer_text: An immediate action from my recently published SEN Reform Agenda considers the Strategic Area Planning for special education provision across the continuum of provision, based on projected need, ensuring full pathways across all key stages and a SEN-first approach to operational plans. The Education Authority’s Operational Plan 2 (2024-26) prioritises special education provision, including a review of all special schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of further support his Department could provide in the process of obtaining a statement of special educational needs for a child.
answer_text: The Department of Education, alongside the Education Authority (EA), has lead responsibility for the statutory assessment and statementing process for special educational needs (SEN), Health plays a key role. This includes commitments in the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan to streamline the Statutory Assessment and Review (SARS) process to meet statutory timeframes.The Department, Public Health Agency (PHA) and HSC Trusts continue to work actively to identify the needs of children and young people at the earliest possible stage and to respond in a timely manner in providing HSC input as a necessary part of the SARS process. This is facilitated through close interdepartmental cooperation and a shared focus on improving outcomes for children and families.The PHA are working closely with HSC Trusts and EA to streamline the statutory assessment and review processes, including improving the secure transfer of relevant information across sectors and enhancing the coordination between health and education professionals. These steps are aimed at reducing delays and ensuring a more integrated and responsive service.The PHA remains committed to working with all partners including parents and children and young people to support the timely provision of advice to ensure that support for children with SEN enhances their ability to access the curriculum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how he intends to work with the Minister for Infrastructure in relation to people having access to the health estate via public and community transport.
answer_text: In 2024, my Department launched a public consultation - 'Hospitals — Creating a Network for Better Outcomes' which included a section on the importance of having in place suitable transport arrangements to underpin the hospital network. I recognise the potential need for additional travel for some is carefully balanced against the need to provide more timely access to care and a more efficient service, which is better able to meet the health needs of the entire population. The provision of transport is a cross-departmental issue, and I have already been engaging with the Minister for Infrastructure and the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to discuss the full range of transport options available.  We instructed officials to recommission the Cross-Departmental Working Group on Community Transport to explore how to provide the best, value for money services.  The Group continues to meet frequently with representatives considering two key issues: to maximise the benefits of the current services provided by community transport using existing resources (and whether any more resources are available); andto maximise the future potential of community transport.This Group have been tasked to provide further options for Ministerial consideration, in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an updated timeframe of phase 2 of the community transport review; and (ii) to outline any challenges her Department foresees in completion of this phase of the review.
answer_text: The initial findings of the Phase 2 Review of Community Transport schemes, funded by the Department, are due by autumn 2025. The primary challenge to completion of the review is the volume of work to be undertaken within a relatively short time period by a small project team.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on phase 2 of the community transport review.
answer_text: Phase 2 of the Community Transport review project is on track to deliver initial findings by autumn. 2025. The review is focused on three main workstreams.Finance & Performance.Service Delivery ModelsFunding Models.The review is currently considering the finance and performance data for the community transport providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what consideration has been given to social return on investment models in relation to (i) public; and (ii) community, transport as part of the forthcoming transport strategy.
answer_text: ANSWER IS DEPENDENT ON ORAL AQO 2051/22-27 : To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Transport Strategy 2035.The Transport Strategy will set a vision and strategic priorities for transport up to 2035 and will recognise the important roles public transport and community transport services offer for healthy, connected and prosperous communities.The draft Strategy is a high-level strategic document that will inform future decision-making. It will not dictate the assessment methods to be used in determining transport measures. This is a matter for subsequent down-stream decision making processes. I announced to the Assembly on the 02 June my intention to publish the draft Transport Strategy in the coming weeks.  I encourage everyone to engage in the consultation when it launches to help shape a transport system that is safe, efficient, and inclusive, ensuring that everyone, regardless of their location, income, or ability can access the opportunities they need to thrive.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the impact that tariffs on goods entering the United States of America will have on an all-island economy.
answer_text: We recognise that the announcement of tariffs by the United States of America is of concern to businesses here, who ultimately need certainty in their trading arrangements. The EU and UK are still considering their responses. We are working closely with the Minister for the Economy, whose officials are carrying out detailed analysis on the impacts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any consideration has been given to displaying updated road death statistics on existing electronic road safety messaging across the road network.
answer_text: My Department operates numerous Variable Message Signs (VMS) across the north, each with a set number of characters and lines that can be used to display messaging to the travelling public. Electronic Variable message signs (EVMS) enable the Department for Infrastructure to meet their obligations as a network operator with regard to incident management, reducing congestion, informing motorists, improving network performance and ensuring the safety of road users and workforce. The primary purpose of Variable Message Signs is to inform and advise road users about emergencies, incidents and network management.  They are also aimed at improving road safety and minimising the impact of congestion. At agreed times and durations, through liaison with DfI Road Safety Education and PSNI, EVMS are also used to display messages to support national road safety campaigns. These messages are used to reinforce positive road behaviours from motorists such as “belting up” or “slowing down”.I have asked my officials to consider adding road death statistics to these road safety campaigns.All road safety message legends must have supporting legislation and authorisation of Road Traffic Regulation (NI) Order 1997.  Currently there are 39 approved safety messages.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the contribution his Department can make to enabling housebuilding.
answer_text: I welcome the Executive’s recent endorsement of the Housing Supply Strategy as one of the main drivers for increasing housing stock, particularly social housing, as a key priority in our Programme for Government.I have given my commitment to Minister Lyons for my Officials to continue engaging with the Department for Communities, as part of the recently established Housing Supply Strategy Project Board, noting that my Officials continue to be involved in the Department for Communities Rural Housing Steering Group.I appreciate the importance of this Strategy and the need to collectively work across the Executive to determine what solutions or pathways could be put in place to unlock housing.  This includes importantly, giving due regard for housing provision in rural areas as per the Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016, to enable and support thriving, sustainable and resilient rural communities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of (i) the first Assured Skills Academy; and (ii) the impact that the 29 academy graduates will have on her Department.
answer_text: The first Professional and Technical (P&T) Skills Academy for Trainee Civil Engineering Assistants (TCEAs) launched in July 2024 as a pre-employment training course designed to equip candidates with the initial skills necessary for commencing a fulfilling career in the Civil Engineering field within my department. The Academy is an innovative approach to employment as it creates an alternative pathway to attract and upskill individuals, and provides a route to a guaranteed interview for a job vacancy within the Department for Infrastructure A Post Project Evaluation has commenced, and early indications show that the Academy has been successful in providing relevant and valuable training in advance of employment, offering fully supported learning for candidates, and is an example of how the NICS is using innovative recruitment models. The Academy offered both full-time and part-time pathways, providing flexibility for individuals who were juggling other responsibilities.My Department has been facing significant recruitment challenges, and the Skills Academy is seen a viable pathway to address these issues. The Academy has provided a new recruitment pipeline into the Department, aiming to grow and foster talent from the ground up. Those who completed the Skills Academy and were successful at interview are employed as TCEAs. They have now started their careers within DfI Transport and Road Asset Management and have already been enrolled in a BTEC Level 3 National Diploma in Civil Engineering. A clear career pathway for exists for these TCEAs, ensuring that they have opportunities for growth and development throughout their careers. I fully expect the graduates of the P&T Skills Academy to contribute significantly to various projects within my Department, improving the daily lives of all the people we serve.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the need to include logistics-related content in core subjects such as Maths, ICT and Sciences in the school curriculum, in a bid to address ongoing logistics workforce shortages.
answer_text: I have commissioned a strategic review of the curriculum, currently being carried out by Lucy Crehan, which will focus on the purpose, design, specification and implementation of the Curriculum and how we can learn from high performing education systems to bring about necessary reform. Ms Crehan will provide her report to my Department by the end of April, at which point I will consider the recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on his engagement with Treasury since taking office.
answer_text: In my introductory call with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury on 5 February I raised the significant pressure the Executive is facing as a result of the British Government’s decision to increase employer National Insurance contributions. I raised it again at the Finance Inter-ministerial Standing Committee (F:ISC) meeting in Cardiff on 27 February. There was a very frank exchange at this meeting, with my counterparts in Scotland and Wales facing similar issues. Whitehall departments will be fully funded for the additional costs they will incur because of the increase. However, due to difference in the structure of our public services the Barnett consequentials received as result of this funding will fall far short of the costs facing the three devolved governments. It is unacceptable that a policy decision by the British Government, should result in a financial pressure for the devolved governments. Their failure to meet these costs in full will have a negative effect on public services here. The F:ISC focused on other areas of joint interest including the upcoming second phase of the Spending Review and the changes to Agriculture Property Relief.I also meet with the CST for a bilateral meeting on the same day. I took this opportunity to raise a number of further issues with him including the ongoing dispute in relation to Victims payments, the exceptional costs facing the Executive in relation to legal challenges, the potential funding cliff edge in 2026-27, NIHE borrowing, tax free childcare, hospitality VAT, Agricultural Support funds and Casement Park.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any measures she will take to unlock capacity in the wastewater system for housing.
answer_text: I recognise the impact that the current wastewater capacity challenges are having on the delivery of housing growth and I will continue to build on the previous Minister’s three pronged approach that is already in place.  This includes working with Executive colleagues to increase wastewater investment, exploring options for developer contributions, with a consultation document to be launched in March 2025, and introducing a Water, Flooding and Sustainable Drainage Bill to the Assembly.During this financial year, NI Water has been allocated a budget of almost £0.5 billion, which is just under 40% of the total non-ring-fenced budget available for my entire Department.From the recent October monitoring round, NI Water received a further £32m, of which £19.5m is to fund targeted action to release wastewater constraints.As a result of this, NI Water has already been able to progress projects across Antrim, Derry and Tyrone, which will unlock sewerage capacity for 2,300 homes and properties.  I plan to continue that focus on unlocking capacity and can confirm that my officials are working collaboratively with colleagues in DAERA and DfC to contribute to the aims and objectives of the Executive’s Housing Supply Strategy.   Finally, I can confirm that my Department has bid for Transformation funding over the next 4 years to progress a pilot project on nature-based drainage solutions. The aim of the project is to pilot the development, delivery and operation of a range of different, nature-based drainage and flood management interventions to demonstrate their effectiveness in managing the flow of water through our urban areas and helping to reduce flood risk, and pollution whilst adapting to the impacts of climate change.  Blue / green drainage infrastructure is cleaner and greener and can help slow the flow of water entering our sewers, helping to free up capacity, as well as reducing out of sewer flooding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Energy Efficiency Capital Grant scheme.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of dental services in Newry and Armagh following the privatisation of Keady Dental Surgery; and (ii) to detail any plans he has to ensure services remain accessible for all patients.
answer_text: Keady Dental Surgery has not informed my Department of any plans to move to a privatised model.  All three General Dental Service (GDS) dentists currently registered with this practice are continuing to provide GDS treatment. Practices may provide dental services to a combination of Health Service and private patients.  No information is held centrally in relation to services provided to private patients.The information on the number of Health Service dental practices, whose dentists have made arrangements with my Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) (previously the Health and Social Care Board) to provide Health Service dentistry in Northern Ireland is routinely published on a quarterly basis in our Accredited Official Statistics publication: FPS General Dental Services Statistics Quarterly Update. The number of dental practices, including orthodontic, with a practice address in the Newry and Armagh Assembly Area at 31 December 2024, the most recent publicly available statistics, is 29. Keady Dental Surgery is included in these figures.  This information can be found in Table 7.3 at: https://bso.hscni.net/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/dental_quarterly_tables_q3_24-25_published.xlsx General Dental Practitioners (GDPs) are independent contractors and, under their existing contract, are under no obligation to register new Health Service patients and are able to decide which patients they will treat under Health Service arrangements. Like-wise the General Dental Services (GDS) Regulations do not place an obligation on dental practitioners to provide any form of care to non-registered patients. For many months now, my Department has been trying to address these issues with increased funding and initiatives aimed at increasing access to Health Service dental care for both registered and unregistered patients. In March 2024 a £9.2m investment package for GDS was announced, including re-establishment of the Enhanced Child Examination (ECE) Scheme, a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for certain priority treatments for 2024/25, and the establishment of a Dental Access Scheme (DAS). This is aimed at improving access (during the working week) for unregistered patients experiencing an emergency, urgent or pressing dental condition. There are 29 practices participating in the scheme, 7 of which are located in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust area. Currently, DAS is providing access to dental care for around 500 patients each week.From June to December 2024, over 22,400 newly registered children have benefitted from the ECE Scheme.Analyses carried out to date indicate that these measures have had a positive impact and my Department will continue to monitor these schemes closely. I want to reassure the public and the dental profession that dentistry is being prioritised within my Department and that informed consideration is being given to the long-term strategic aims for GDS. In the interim, our key priorities continue to

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions his Department is carrying out to assist local councils combat illegal dumping.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) has assisted District Councils in a range of activities to help prevent illegal dumping. These include:The erection of suitable barriers and signage;Joint inspection visits to businesses; and The production and distribution of promotional material, including guidance leaflets and online videos regarding illegal dumping.The NIEA also supports District Councils by regularly contributing to joint working groups to share experience and best practice, for example: The Northern Ireland Environmental Quality Forum.In January 2025 NIEA’s Environmental Crime Unit supported the District’s Councils by delivering training on NIEA’s and District Council’s joint Fly Tipping Protocol and effective use of legislation to support enforcement against illegal dumping.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister for the Economy regarding NI Water connections relating to Invest NI projects.
answer_text: Whilst I have not met with the Minister for the Economy specifically regarding NI Water connections relating to Invest NI projects, the management of public water and wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water, therefore, NI Water would liaise directly with Invest NI on any such matters.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the progress of Local Development Plans.
answer_text: All councils are currently preparing their new Local Development Plans (LDPs) to provide the spatial framework for development for their areas over a 15-year period.To date four councils have now adopted their Plan Strategies (PS), and two further councils have received the Department’s direction to adopt their PS with modifications (Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council (ANBC) and Derry City and Strabane District Council (DCSDC)). The council timetables indicate that 3 further councils will publish their draft Plan Strategies (dPS) later this year.  Officials continue to engage with those councils preparing for the publication of their dPS and draft Local Policy Plans (dLPP).    I am, of course aware that in recent times there have been increasing concerns about both the length of time LDPs are taking to adopt and the resourcing issues that exist within the Planning Appeals Commission. Whilst the power to use an appointed person exists to conduct and IE it has not been utilised as the PAC are the specified body set up in legislation to deal with such matters. However, I am committed to ensuring that our planning system operates in an efficient and effective way and you will be aware that my Department recently submitted a bid to the Interim Public Sector Transformation Board. The Department’s bid included seeking funding for independent examiners for Local Development Plan Independent Examinations and for Public Local Inquiries and Hearings for Departmental planning applications. This would allow us to help address the current backlogs in such work and allow us to consider new approaches to commissioning this work in the future.I am awaiting confirmation from the Board if the bid has been successful.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has any plans to consider natural drainage solutions for flooding and issues with wastewater infrastructure.
answer_text: My Department has been actively promoting the use of natural drainage solutions through various strategies and plans.‘Sustainable Water’, A Long-Term Water Strategy for Northern Ireland covers the period 2015 – 2040, and takes a holistic view of how water can be more sustainably managed through our environment. My Department’s Flood Risk Management Plan for the period 2021 – 2027 promotes the use of natural flood management (NFM) interventions, such as upland storage, to be considered on all flood alleviation schemes to complement the traditional hard engineered solutions. The Living With Water Plans in Belfast and Derry advocate a more integrated approach to drainage and flood risk management in both cities and identify opportunities for reducing the pressure on our existing drainage and wastewater systems by retrofitting NFM interventions, as well as sustainable drainage systems (SuDS). To this end, my Department has made a bid to the Interim Public Sector Transformation Board for funding to progress a pilot project on nature-based drainage solutions. The aim of the project is to pilot the development, delivery and operation of a number of different, nature-based SuDS and NFM interventions to demonstrate their effectiveness in managing the flow of water through our urban areas and helping to reduce flood risk, and pollution whilst adapting to the impacts of climate change.  Nature based drainage solutions also have an important role to play in new development. As I have previously outlined, I have secured Executive agreement for the Water, Flooding and Sustainable Drainage Bill to be introduced to the Assembly. This legislation will provide powers to encourage increased use of sustainable drainage systems which use nature to separate and release storm water more slowly into the system so that it does not overload the existing drainage networks and wastewater treatment works.  Through these strategies and plans I hope to deliver a move towards the use of nature-based solutions on a larger scale.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the development of the Children and Young People's Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what work is being done to realise the full potential of the Maze Long Kesh site.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has given consideration to legislation in relation to the use of e-scooters in public spaces.
answer_text: In the absence of any enabling legislation, it remains illegal to use e-scooters on public roads and public spaces here. I currently have no plans to change that position. It also remains illegal to use e-scooters on public roads or in public places in Britain unless they are being used as part of the ongoing Department for Transport (DfT) trial of rental e-scooters scheme. This scheme has been extended until May 2026 and DfT may introduce legislation to enable a more general use of e-scooters there once the pilot scheme is complete and its outcomes assessed. You may be aware that legislation was introduced in the South in May 2024 making the use of some e-scooters legal on public roads, subject to specified restrictions. Users must be 16 or older and obey a speed limit of 20km/h. Among other restrictions, e-scooters can’t be used on footpaths or in public places and users can't carry passengers.To help inform the way forward here, my officials will continue to monitor the progress of the trial scheme in Britain as well as the impact of the recent legislative changes in the South.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on the twenty-eighth Plenary meeting of the North South Ministerial Council, held on 13 September 2024.
answer_text: The 28th Plenary meeting of the North South Ministerial Council was held in Dublin Castle on 13 September 2024.We had good discussion with the Irish Government on areas where we are working together to deliver benefits to both jurisdictions.		Among the items we discussed were:Trade and Business matters;Cross-Border New, Decade, New Approach Commitments;Climate Change and Loss of Biodiversity; andMother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene laundries and workhouses.A statement to the House on the meeting was provided on 12 November 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the action Irish medium schools who are oversubscribed should take if there is not suitable accommodation to meet the demands in Irish medium provision in their area.
answer_text: If an Irish Medium school has an approved Development Proposal (DP) to increase enrolment, my Department will consider the provision of any additional accommodation required to meet the needs of the approved DP, subject to the necessary approvals, competing priorities and available funding.The Department ensures schools have the accommodation required to safely accommodate pupils. If a school has concerns about their accommodation, they should submit an unavoidable minor works application to either the Department of Education, for non-controlled schools, or the Education Authority, for controlled schools. The application will be reviewed to determine if the school is eligible for replacement or additional accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how his Department is working with veterinarians, scientists and stakeholders to ensure the bovine TB programme is based on the latest evidence.
answer_text: Earlier this year, I asked Brian Dooher, Chief Veterinary Officer (CVO) to undertake a thorough review of matters relating to bTB programme and policy, including the actions contained in the Bovine TB Eradication Strategy.  This review has seen engagement take place with veterinary organisations such as North of Ireland Veterinary Association, the Association of Veterinary Surgeons Practicing in Northern Ireland, and the British Veterinary Association (NI).   Brian has also met with a wide range of stakeholders including Animal Health and Welfare NI, the Diary Council, the Ulster Farmers’ Union, the Northern Ireland Agricultural Producers’ Association, Ulster Wildlife, the USPCA and the Northern Ireland Badger Group, amongst many others, to seek views and to hear the latest evidence on how bTB might be successfully tackled here.  The CVO has discussed bovine TB with the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute and colleagues in neighbouring jurisdictions to ensure that we are fully up to date on work currently taking place and that our TB Programme is based on the latest evidence and best practices.Alongside this, my Department is also working to ensure that its bibliography of scientific papers on wildlife intervention is up to date and this work has been shared with stakeholders who have been asked to review and add to the peer reviewed papers included in the bibliography.I am looking forward to considering the CVO’s findings in the coming weeks and I intend to publish a new delivery plan well before Christmas.  This plan will clearly set out our priorities over the remainder of this Assembly mandate and will reinvigorate our efforts to support farmers in tackling this costly disease.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the next phase of the Ulster Canal project.
answer_text: The Ulster Canal project is being progressed in three phases. Phase 1, which is Lough Erne to Castle Saunderson and Phase 2 which is Clones to Clonfad, are both now complete and open for navigation. Work on the third and final phase of Castle Saunderson to Clonfad is in progress. Feasibility and pre-construction work, including vesting of lands and renewing planning permission is underway.Phase 3 is subdivided into 4 sub phases and the current estimated completion date of all sub phases is 2028-29.I am fully committed to supporting the completion of this ambitious project which is transformational to communities and businesses in the region.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline their plan to consult on a Programme for Government.

To ask the Minister of Health how his Department has adapted the emotional and wellbeing framework to adjust to (i) the Autism Act (NI) 2011; and (ii) the Autism (Amendment) Act (NI) 2022, which both require the framing of distinct autism services, separate data collection, research and funding reports.
answer_text: The HSC Children’s Emotional health and Wellbeing (EHWB) Framework has considered requirements of both the Autism Act (NI) 2011; and the Autism (Amendment) Act (NI) 2022. It will help align early help and support to children and young people waiting for an assessment on an autism pathway both pre and post diagnosis.The primary emphasis of current service provision lies in the diagnostic assessment pathways, which are considered as a means to access assistance and support. The objective of the EHWB Framework is to prioritise access to and provision of support based on the individual's immediate needs, rather than waiting on a diagnosis before offering help. It outlines the principles of inclusivity, shared language, and equal access to services. While individuals diagnosed with autism may require specific support, their diagnosis should not hinder their access to inclusive universal health services. The Framework advocates for a workforce that is autism and neurodiversity aware including expanding community and voluntary support that is knowledgeable and supportive of autistic people. Implementation of the Framework will uphold and complement exclusive autism services, rather than replacing them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the role of the Independent Autism Reviewer will comply with the duties set out in the Autism (Amendment) Act (NI) 2022.
answer_text: The role of the Independent Reviewer will comply with the duties set out in the Autism (Amendment) Act (NI) 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why his Department has not set measurable targets in (i) the Autism Strategy 2023-2028; and (ii) the Delivery Plan 2023-25, against which its effectiveness may be assessed in compliance with clause 3A(5) of the Autism (Amendment) Act (NI) 2022.
answer_text: At the time of the development of the Autism Strategy 2023-2028, Northern Ireland government departments were experiencing unprecedented budgetary challenges including planning within singular year budget allocations that placed some constraints upon initial development and implementation of this strategy.  In consideration of these constraints, and to ensure that provision of support and services could continue to be delivered the initial two-year Delivery Plan 2023-2025 commenced implementation of this strategy.  The Delivery Plan, which was published on the Department’s website, sets out the actions to be achieved against commitments, timeframes associated outcomes and the delivery partners involved. A monitoring framework has been established to drive implementation. The monitoring framework will collect information on how outcomes have been measured and I will ensure that the Assembly are provided with an update on the first-year delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of his Department's non-compliance with Clause 3C of the Autism (Amendment) Act (NI) 2022 by not securing an expenses-only appointment or to provide the budget to fund the part-time post of an independent Autism Reviewer.
answer_text: I am aware of the responsibility to appoint an Independent Autism Reviewer for Northern Ireland and acknowledge the significant role this post will bring.  However, as the Executive budget set on 25 April 24 will result in seriousand potentially irreparable damage to health and social care services  I voted against the budget on that basis and now must consider cuts to frontline services.  My Department is now working towards the publication of an EQIA on its budget allocation.  Whilst it will be necessary to make some decisions on allocations prior to the conclusion of the consultation period responses will be taken into account in prioritising the allocation of any additional funding received in year.  I have therefore deferred a decision on the Reviewer until I can consider Health funding pressures in the round.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) his Department’s plan to comply with Clause 2 (4E) of the Autism (Amendment) Act (NI) 2022 which requires his Department to provide a plan to reduce waiting times for autism assessment and treatment services provided by Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) how this will address the issue of families paying for costly private diagnosis and follow-up intervention.
answer_text: Waiting lists for autism assessment remain a challenge. Many children or young people wait up to three years for an assessment; for adults many will wait up to seven years for an assessment. The Department acknowledges that this is not acceptable and can have a profound impact on a person’s mental health. To address this SPPG colleagues have in recent months been meeting with all Trusts to determine the existing referral and assessment routes for paediatric autism services.  Officials are collaborating with colleagues from Public Health Agency and Community Child Health to establish a standardised pathway across the region that aligns to NICE guidelines and can better identify workforce requirements. Consistent pathways will allow for better planned management of current waiting lists and ongoing demand. SPPG have met with PHA and Child Health colleagues and it was agreed that the development of consistent pathways will be taken forward through the Community Children’s Social Care Group jointly chaired by SPPG and PHA.  Pathways will take a broader neurodevelopmental approach, incorporating a regionally consistent Children and Young Peoples Referral Service. This will be critical to managing referrals and waiting lists going forward. The development of consistent regional pathways will help ensure no one is disadvantaged anywhere in the region. The implementation of the HSC Children’s Emotional health and Wellbeing (EHWB) Framework will help align early help and support to children and young people waiting for an assessment on an autism pathway.  It will also help identify how future investment can best meet the needs of local populations. Current service provision is focused on diagnostic pathways which are seen as a gateway to help and support. The EHWB Framework focuses on providing support to meet presenting need rather than waiting for, or being reliant on a diagnosis. This will hopefully reduce the pressure on services users to seek private diagnosis as help and support will be more readily available both pre and post diagnosis.  A Review of Adult Autism provision across the region has been undertaken and the recommendations were presented to the Permanent Secretary.  I am aware of the responsibility to appoint an Independent Autism Reviewer for Northern Ireland and acknowledge the significant role this post will bring in advocating for autistic people and their families. I have therefore asked officials to provide advice on how this appointment may now be progressed so I can consider this more fully and in a timely manner.One of the recommendations is the appointment of a Lead for Adult Autism provision - recruitment of this post is currently underway. This role will be responsible for taking forward the recommendations emerging from the review; this will include the management of waiting lists for adult autism assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the measures his Department will take to improve road safety.
answer_text: As Minister for Infrastructure part of my responsibility is promoting and improving road safety. This is a responsibility I take very seriously - every death or serious injury is one too many. I am aware that on the weekend of 23 March 2024, five people were killed in two separate road traffic collisions within your constituency and I extend my sympathies to everyone affected. In October 2022 I published the draft Road Safety Strategy for Northern Ireland to 2030. This is supported by an action plan for 2023/24 containing 52 action measures. As this is a cross-cutting matter I aim to present an updated Road Safety Strategy to the Executive in the coming weeks for their consideration.I am mindful that the 71 deaths on our roads during 2023 was an eight-year high and very aware that more work needs to be done. My officials are currently working with other stakeholders including PSNI, Fire & Rescue Service, and the Ambulance Service to reduce deaths and serious injuries on our roads.For your ease of reference, the draft Road Safety Strategy and associated action plan can be found at the following webpage:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/consultation-outcome-report-new-road-safety-strategy-northern-ireland-2030 One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing road user behaviour. If all of us who use the roads do not change our attitudes our death toll, which has increased in the past year, will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has any plans to reintroduce the Boiler Replacement Scheme.
answer_text: I refer the member to my response to AQW 6960 22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department's efforts to decarbonise the Translink fleet.
answer_text: Translink has commenced the decarbonisation of its bus fleet with 147 zero emission vehicles currently in operation and a further 100 zero emission vehicles due to come into service in 2024/25. Plans for further decarbonisation of the bus fleet is subject to the availability of future funding.The All-Island Strategic Rail Review (AISRR) considers how the rail network on the island of Ireland can contribute to decarbonisation, with two of the Review’s 13 objectives focussed on rail’s contribution to tackling the climate emergency: reducing the carbon emissions associated with rail’s construction, operation, and maintenance; and reducing the carbon emissions from motor vehicle travel by doubling rail’s mode share.In support of these objectives, the draft Review recommends:Developing and implementing an All-Island Rail Decarbonisation Strategy that includes an electrified intercity network. Developing plans to invest in the skills, supply chains, and rolling stock to deliver decarbonisation. Procuring hybrid and electric rolling stock in the medium term.Following closure of the AISRR public consultation in September 2023, work is ongoing to finalise the Review and its recommendations. Once the Review is published, further work will be required to prioritise the recommendations and then to undertake feasibility studies and develop business cases to support future investment decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how his Department will help promote biodiversity.
answer_text: For major road projects, watercourse maintenance and flood alleviation works, my Department not only considers the engineering requirements for the works, but also the impact on biodiversity and the environment.  Where necessary, Environmental Impact Assessments are carried out to assist mitigate as much as possible any potential impacts of the works on the existing environment. In October 2022, I announced a new approach to verge management across the road network to protect the environment. From 2023/24, a single swathe was cut along the verges on the strategic road network twice per year and a similar approach was adopted, for example at the sight lines to junctions, where additional areas of grass may only be cut to maintain visibility, and, on heavier trafficked rural roads. On lighter trafficked rural roads, where possible, the cutting frequency is being reduced to once per year and moved towards the end of the growing season. Rivers Directorate officials liaise with other relevant bodies regarding flood alleviation works and watercourse maintenance programmes, in relation to site specific environmental issues which are then mitigated as much as possible. My Department’s current Social and Environmental Guidance for Water and Sewerage Services states that NI Water should manage its estate to promote biodiversity. In addition, my Department has also been actively promoting the use of Sustainable Drainage Systems (SuDS). SuDS are drainage solutions that utilise a natural approach to managing drainage and often take the form of ponds or swales which promote ecological biodiversity in flora and fauna.My Department also recognises that land use planning will also play an important role in helping to promote biodiversity. The planning system is already fully supportive of measures to conserve and enhance biodiversity. The Department recognises the real benefits of linking people with nature for their health and well-being and it will continue to consider how it and the planning system more generally can continue to play an appropriate part, working with others, to protect and renew nature going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how his Department will promote active travel.
answer_text: I am enthusiastic about the positive change active travel can make to the lives of people who want greater choice in how they travel. I want to help create towns and neighbourhoods that are healthier, more enjoyable places for people to live, work, shop, visit, and invest. I believe that active travel has an important role to play in achieving that.My Department supports a number of important programmes that help young people travel actively, including the Cycling Proficiency Scheme that launched last month and Practical Child Pedestrian Safety Training that is delivered in a number of schools each year, with officials training teachers and providing resources for them to teach their pupils these important life skills. My Department also works in partnership with the Public Health Agency, and co-funds the Active School Travel Programme that is currently delivered by Sustrans. I recognise that with respect to active travel, the key role of my Department is the delivery of high quality infrastructure that enables people to consider walking, wheeling, and cycling as the natural choice for many of their everyday journeys. I am pleased to say that my Department has taken a number of significant steps since the introduction of the Climate Change Act in 2022 to accelerate delivery of active travel infrastructure into the future.We reorganised internally and added key staff to our Active Travel team. We have made important changes to our supply chain and are developing an Active Travel Delivery Plan that includes a full update of our design guidance and proposed active travel network plans for our largest towns and cities. When complete, this plan will complement the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan and the Strategic Plan for Greenways to provide our teams and local councils with a firm basis for the delivery of high-quality active travel for the next ten years and beyond.The Member will appreciate that ambitious delivery of active travel is subject to funding availability, competing priorities within the Department and the Executive, legislative requirements and delivery capacity. I can assure the member that I am committed to putting in place all the measures necessary to allow that to happen.
