To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to list the legislation, relating to areas of responsibility of the Department, that is affected by the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill.
answer_text: My officials are working with Whitehall Departments to finalise a definitive list of Retained EU Law (REUL) with respect to my Department.  It is estimated that approximately 500 pieces of REUL could be affected, however until officials carry out due diligence as to whether the REUL identified is actually caught by the scope of this Bill and the list is finalised, I am not in a position to release any further detail.    In the meantime you can view the Cabinet Office’s REUL Dashboard here: UK Government - Retained EU Law Dashboard | Tableau Public where they have published an initial catalogue of over 2,400 pieces of REUL across 300 unique policy areas and 21 sectors of the economy. While this catalogue is not definitive and does not include all REUL relevant to Devolved Authorities, it will give you an overview of the scope of potential legislation impacted by this Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what work the Department is undertaking to assess the potential impact of the Retained EU Law (Revocation and Reform) Bill, within its areas of responsibility.
answer_text: My officials are currently engaging with colleagues in the Executive Office, legal advisers and officials in Whitehall to understand the full implications of this Bill and its potential impact on my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) how many farms in the north farm fish; and (ii) what legislation governs these farms.
answer_text: There are currently 32 active licensed fish farms in Northern Ireland (13 finfish and 19 shellfish farms); andFish farms are licensed by my Department under The Fisheries Act (Northern Ireland) 1966.  Fish farms are also approved and monitored by my Department for aquatic health and disease control purposes.  The legislation governing this is Regulation (EU) 2016/429 and The Aquatic Animal Heath Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2009.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Narrow Water Bridge project.
answer_text: My officials are working closely with the Irish government, Louth County Council and other stakeholders to deliver the Narrow Water Bridge project as quickly as possible. The required Bridge Orders were made by my Department on 24 February 2022 and became operational on 26 March 2022.Louth County Council, as the lead project partner, has recently published a Prior Information Notice to provide advanced notice of the tender process to appoint a contractor. Subject to completion of ongoing development work and necessary approvals, it is currently envisaged that the tender process will commence later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on how his Department is enhancing all-Ireland connectivity.
answer_text: I am fully committed to improving connectivity across the island and I will be working with my counterparts in Dublin on a number of all-island projects, all of which are commitments within ‘New Decade New Approach’. I am committed to collaborating to improve the lives of all our citizens and share ambitions on public transport, promoting active travel, shaping our places to live, and tackling the climate emergency.  As you may be aware, work is ongoing to take a strategic look at our rail network to determine how best it can deliver for our citizens in the future. One element of this work is the All-Island Strategic Rail Review. The Review is being undertaken to allow us to consider our rail network across this island with a view to how it can be improved for everyone. The Review will examine the potential for rail connections across the island including how our existing connections could be improved and where new rail links would be best placed. It will also examine how we can connect our rail network to areas across the island of Ireland, our international gateways through our ports and airports, and the potential for transporting freight using the rail network, whilst also considering how we can move to a low or zero emission rail network. It is anticipated that the Review will be completed in the autumn of 2022.My officials are also working closely with the Irish government, Louth County Council and other stakeholders to deliver the important Narrow Water Bridge Project as quickly as possible. The required Bridge Orders were made by my Department on 24 February 2022 and became operational on 26 March 2022.  Louth County Council, as the lead project partner, has recently published a Prior Information Notice to provide advanced notice of the tender process to appoint a contractor. Subject to completion of ongoing development work and necessary approvals it is currently envisaged that the tender process will commence later this year.   The A5 Executive Flagship project is also of significant strategic importance to the region.  This flagship project will not only improve road safety, but will also help to tackle regional imbalance, improve the economy, job prospects and prosperity, and connect communities across the island. A new Environmental Statement Addendum was published on 16 March 2022, commencing a consultation process that concluded on 6 May 2022.   It is hoped that the Public Inquiry can be reconvened in the autumn. Finally, I also welcome progress in relation to the restoration of the Ulster Canal between Lough Erne and Clones. Phase 1 - the stretch between Lough Erne to Castle Saunderson - was completed in spring 2019 and is now open for navigation. Work on Phase 2 - the stretch from Clones to Clonfad - is currently underway.  Phase 3 - the restoration of the stretch from Castle Saunderson to Clonfad - will be completed in four sub-phases. Preparatory work for Phase 3 has commenced with €1m provided from the Shared Island Fund towar

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what his Department is doing to enhance road safety.
answer_text: My Department has a statutory duty to promote road safety and this is a responsibility that I take very seriously. My Department does this through a range of road safety educational activities, engineering and other initiatives. The work carried out by my Department to maintain and improve the road network has an underlying safety aim, such as the renewal of road markings and signage, the provision of traffic signs and pedestrian crossings, resurfacing works and the delivery of major schemes. Our work also includes schemes aimed specifically at improving road safety, including collision remedial schemes which target known problem areas, and traffic calming measures that are primarily aimed at reducing traffic speeds. Street lighting, traffic control and motorway communication systems also play an important role by helping road users navigate our network safely and efficiently.Excessive speed continues to be a problem in some areas and my Department will continue to work with our policing partners to set speed limits that are appropriate for the local conditions.  This year the second tranche of the part-time 20mph speed limits outside schools will be completed meaning that 215 schools will benefit from this initiative over the past two years. Regrettably over 95% of road traffic collisions where someone dies or is seriously injured are due to human error. Therefore, in addition to making proven engineering interventions, my Department also challenges driver behaviour and through its portfolio of road safety campaigns. These campaigns raise awareness of road safety issues and the impact of poor decisions when using the roads and remind people of their responsibility to themselves and other road users. My Department also supports road safety schemes and provides free resources to all schools across the north of Ireland to help embed good road safety behaviours in our children and young people. I am also currently considering a draft new Road Safety Strategy to 2030 for the North.   The content of the final Strategy will require Executive approval, which is not possible at this time due to the ongoing absence of the Executive.  We will continue working with others including PSNI, Fire and Rescue Service and the Ambulance Service on road safety issues to further reduce deaths and serious injuries on our roads.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department is investing in rural communities.
answer_text: My Department provides investment in rural communities through a number of specific programmes and schemes. This includes progressing a number of major projects to upgrade the strategic road network in rural areas. These include Flagship projects such as A6 Drumahoe to Dungiven, which will open to traffic later this year, and the A5 Western Transport Corridor. There is also a rolling programme of resurfacing across the road network including rural roads. Over the last two years my Department has allocated £29 million to a Roads Recovery Fund, of which £25 million was directed towards rural roads. In addition to this my Department has invested just under £4 million over the past two financial years which will see the introduction of part-time 20mph speed limits at 215 schools, of which around 95 are in rural areas.  In respect of our water and sewerage services, NI Water develops and implements the Rural and Wastewater Investment Programme which aims to improve small rural wastewater treatment works. To date, the programme has provided a total investment of £47 million in rural areas. My Department also subsidises Translink for the provision of the public transport network.  Translink does not report against route types, however, Ulsterbus provides the majority of the rural network, as well as inter-urban services and rural and smaller town services.  My Department further supports all capital investment in our public transport network. Whilst it is not possible to separate this spend on a rural and urban basis, it is clear that improved public transport helps our connectivity across all of the North. In 2021-22, this capital investment was in excess of £240m.My Department also provides funding for eleven Rural Community Transport Partnerships to provide Dial-a-Lift services, and provides investment to rural communities through the Road Safety (Safe Travel) Grant Scheme.Alongside this, my Department is working with the Department for Communities and Department for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs on a COVID Recovery Small Settlements Regeneration Programme and has contributed £6.5m from the Blue Green Infrastructure Fund.  This funding will be used by Local Councils to help connect rural communities and create public spaces that will encourage active travel and enhance our quality of life.  I have just written to Councils inviting them to submit proposals for support for greenway and active travel schemes, such as the Strathfoyle Greenway in Derry, which is due to open late summer 2022, providing the rural communities with the opportunity to travel actively into Derry City Centre.
